name: S1 0.3 - JL

name: S1 0.4 - JR
landmark: Guadalupe St to Santa Fe River Park along Alameda St. Join sidewalk trail W.

name: S1 1.0 - XR
landmark: El Camino Real (sidewalk path) at S St Francis Dr. Cross busy road with caution (traffic light and crosswalk)

name: S1 1.1 - TJ
landmark: Sidewalk to paved Camino Real hiking trail. Trail crosses Santa Fe River on bridge.

name: S1 5.2 - TH
landmark: Developed trail starts up again W of Camino Real TH. CCW cross road then follow dirt roads / swaths / drainage bottom.

name: S1 6.0 - TH
landmark: Trailhead at east end of Romero Park. Leave paved Camino Real hiking trail, join paved Caja del Oro Grant Rd (W).

name: S1 7.3 - JL
landmark: Paved Frontage Rd at Caja del Oro Grant Rd (CR 62). Becomes graded dirt in 0.15 mi (W), where state land begins with possibility for legal camping.

name: S1 8.6 - !!
landmark: Leave state land NW (no camping beyond). (CCW legal camping for about a mile.)

name: S1 10.4 - XR
landmark: Cross CR 62. El Camino Real becomes dirt path NW.

name: S1 10.9 - TH C
landmark: Informal Camino Real TH. Public lands along route again (W). (CCW no camping beyond to SF except for one area of state land.)

name: S1 16.1 - JL (TR>XC) (w2 0.7N)
landmark: El Camino Real at XC or side trail NW. Leave main trail here. Don't follow side trail too long. Proceed to N side of leading ridge to W, picking up wash there W, then constructed trail which switchbacks up to mesa top. (Or to visit Dead Dog Well, 0.7N of route, continue on Camino Real Trail to Dead Dog TH then east on dirt road to corral. Satellite shows large metal cistern and a trough in corral with cows. Recent imagery shows wet (older imagery looks dry).)

name: S1 16.3 - (XC>TR)
landmark: Pick up trail around here, which switchbacks up to mesa top to W. From there, continue W XC to next waypoint at road.

name: S1 16.6 - JR (XC>RD)
landmark: Join 2-track NW.

name: S1 16.7 - JL
landmark: 2-track junction, turn left (SW).

name: S1 18.4 - JR
landmark: 2-track road at better dirt road (NW).

name: S1 21.0 - JR JL
landmark: Dirt road junction. Pass thru fence at cattleguard.

name: S1 22.1 - JL JR
landmark: Left / S briefly on powerline track and through gate, then right / W onto another track.

name: S1 23.0 - JR
landmark: 2-track junction, keep right / W.

name: S1 24.2 - JR JL
landmark: 2-track junction. Right / NW here, then left / W in 0.15mi.

name: S1 26.1 - JL
landmark: Left fork / W. Unofficial start of Frijoles Overlook Trail, although remains a 4WD road for now.

name: S1 26.7 - RD>TR
landmark: Frijoles Overlook Trail becomes a faint singletrack / game trail to N here as it descends steeply off the rim of White Rock Canyon toward the Rio Grande. 1000ft total elevation loss. May become essentially XC, especially the last 200'. Aim for next waypoint at suggested fording spot as needed.

name: S1 27.8 - (S1A START)
landmark: Main route (XC) meets start of Frijoles Rim Alt (also XC here). Goal of alt is to exit White Rock Cyn via ridge to W of Frijoles Cyn, thus offering a significant shortcut vs the main route (10 mi shorter, 4+ mi of which are paved on main route). Alt initially very steep and loose (but proven manageable - decide when you get here). No viable route exists up Frijoles since flooding after 2011 Las Conchas Fire took out the trail in the lower canyon (dangerous, impassable obstructions / erosion). If staying on main route, continue XC NE along alluvial plain of Frijoles confluence, near banks of Rio.

name: S1 32.3 - JL
landmark: Ancho Rapids Tr ends here at Powerline Point Tr. Head NW.

name: S1 32.9 - (S1B END)
landmark: End of 13.2 mi long White Rock Resupply Loop (jct with Broken Mesa Tr here). Continue NW on Powerline Point Tr.

name: S1 33.8 - TH JL (TR>RD)
landmark: Powerline Point Tr (old closed road aka 'trail') ends at parking area along NM Hwy 4. Turn left / W onto the paved 2-lane road. Route has to remain on hwy until entering Bandelier N.M. 4 mi ahead due to surrounding land owned by Los Alamos National Lab (strictly off limits with lots of signs and fences). It's a pretty walk but the shoulder can be narrow so stay alert. In places where shoulder is wider, can avoid walking pavement at times but remain on public / hwy side of any fences.

name: S1 38.1 - JL JR
landmark: Leave NM Hwy 4 at long last, left / SW onto Bandelier National Monument entrance road, then within 0.2 turn right on road to Juniper CG. You won't pass an entrance station but should pay the fee when you get near the visitor center farther ahead. No camping within Bandelier except in backcountry (or at campgrounds, space dependent). Get required free backcountry permit at VC ahead.

name: S1 40.4 - JL
landmark: Frey Tr ends here at Pueblo Loop Tr., which is very much worth exploring in full to learn about the ruins and history at Bandelier. Before heading farther afield, such as to Alcove House or into the Bandelier backcountry, continue on the main route (S) to the visitor center to pay entrance fee and get camping permit.

name: S1 41.0 - JR (S1A END w3 0.25E)
landmark: Jct main route and end of 2.8 mi long Frijoles Rim Alternate. (Water and overnight permits from Bandelier visitor center 0.25E. Those having taken the alternate should also pay the entrance fee at VC).

name: S1 42.1 - JL
landmark: Jct Long Tr / Frijoles Rim Tr / Middle Alamo Tr. Turn left (W) on Middle Alamo. (CCW follow Long Trail SE.) Middle Alamo produces a longer and more rewarding route across Bandelier than would following Frijoles Rim.

name: S1 46.2 - JR
landmark: Jct Middle Alamo Tr and Tierra Media Tr. Main route remains on Middle Alamo.

name: S1 46.7 - !!
landmark: Yapashi Pueblo interpretive site. Yapashi was inhabited from 1200 and 1500AD by the Keres people, and at its peak was a major village, with several hundred rooms, and structures up to three stories high. Potsherds are numerous. (Please take only pictures.)

name: S1 47.2 - !!
landmark: Stone Lions Shrine. Highly unique prehistoric ceremonial site featuring a series of carved stone lions within a rock amphitheater. The statues, while eroded, still bear some resemblance to mountain lions, and presented in a crouching or sleeping posture.

name: S1 47.4 - JR
landmark: Upper Alamo Tr at Hondo Tr. Main route remains on Upper Alamo. (A 7 mi round-trip hike off-route to renowned and remote Painted Cave pictograph site is possible via Hondo and Lower Capulin trails.)

name: S1 51.1 - JL
landmark: Upper Alamo Tr ends at Frijoles Rim Tr. Left (W) on Frijoles Rim.

name: S1 51.6 - TJ (S1C END)
landmark: Main route at west end of 7.5 mi long Frijoles Canyon Alt. Main route leaves Frijoles Rim Tr, continuing west on trail connecting to old Sawyer Mesa Rd beyond the park boundary.

name: S1 52.0 - camping
landmark: Bandelier N.M. boundary. USFS lands to west, with legal permit-less camping. Trail / old road may be increasingly hard to follow since Las Conchas Fire of 2011.

name: S1 57.9 - JR
landmark: Graded dirt road junction. FR 287 / Sawyer Mesa Rd at FR 89 / St Peter's Dome Rd. Heading N, soon leave Santa Fe NF back into Bandelier for a bit (no camping).

name: S1 58.5 - JL TH
landmark: St Peter's Dome Rd at Alamo Boundary Tr.

name: S1 59.4 - !!
landmark: Bandelier N.M. at Valles Caldera National Preserve boundary. No camping within the Preserve and only certain areas are open for hiking without paying entrance fee. Our route only remains for several miles and should be logistically easy enough for hikers to deal with.

name: S1 59.5 - JL
landmark: Head NW on Coyote Call Tr.

name: S1 60.9 - JL
landmark: Leave marked trail W onto a combo of old 2-track / swath / xc. Goal is to avoid walking nearby paved NM 4.

name: S1 62.5 - JR JL
landmark: End x-c (S) at FR 286. Then follow NM 4 (S) briefly to next wpt.

name: S1 62.6 - JR
landmark: NM 4 at Valles Caldera entrance road. Gate is open most days, daylight hours only. No camping or night travel advised, but should be fine to enter on foot whether gate is locked or not.

name: S1 63.2 - JL TH
landmark: Missing Cabin / Hidden Valley TH. Follow trail W past remains of old cabin once used as part of a movie set. The trail then drops down a small hill to follow the E Fork Jemez R. as it flows toward SW corner of the preserve, following a narrow valley and passing interesting volcanic formations. Some parts of the trail may be marshy. Backcountry permit not needed for this hike and entrance fee seems to only apply to vehicles parking at or beyond entrance station (which is another half mile N along entrance road).

name: S1 71.7 - XR
landmark: E Fork Tr 137 crosses over NM Hwy 4

name: S1 73.1 - TJ CG
landmark: Jct within Jemez Falls Campground. Head S 0.3 mi via side trail to overlook of popular and scenic Jemez Falls.

name: S1 73.4 - JR !! (S1D START)
landmark: Watch carefully for a junction here and turn right onto old road / track / swath, likely unmarked, leaving Trail 137 for good. Alternatively, remain on Trail 137 in order to follow the 2.8 mi mapped alternate that runs by inviting McCauley Warm Spring. It's inviting because it's warm (not overly hot), consists of several nice soaking pools, and is located far enough into the backcountry to avoid the crowds, at least most weekdays (spring or fall). This alternate then leaves Trail 137 itself at another vague junction and climbs adventurously back to higher ground (after losing a fair bit of elevation to reach the spring). You'll have to decide whether it's worth the effort to take that dip!

name: S1 73.7 - JL !!
landmark: Approx jct old 2-track road and swath climbing out of canyon to left / W. Watch carefully. May be more of an xc affair here to next wpt.

name: S1 74.3 - JR (S1D END)
landmark: Old dirt road junction at end of 2.8 mi long McCauley Warm Spring Alt. Main route continues right / NW. (If coming from alt, turn left.)

name: S1 76.5 - JL
landmark: Dirt road junction. Turn left / NW. Don't join the highway here.

name: S1 76.8 - !!
landmark: Jemez Canyon overlook. Various side trails, some paved, head W to canyon rim.

name: S1 77.0 - TH JL
landmark: Parking for Jemez Canyon Overlook. Turn left (NW) on NM Hwy 4, then left again within 200 yds onto closed road network, following it W and N.

name: S1 77.6 - JL
landmark: 2-track junction. Follow road W, eventually may become xc. Goal is to follow ridge W at edge of forest. Drops steeply to S with cliffs, so stay generally north of that and eventually descend steeply back down to Hwy 4 just S of La Cueva village. If this connection doesn't work out (although some version of it has been verified workable by a NNML hiker), can also just remain on NM 4 from Jemez Canyon overlook area, but it adds about a mile.

name: S2 0.9 - JL
landmark: NM 126 at Horseshoe Spring Rd (W).

name: S2 1.6 - !!
landmark: Local homeowners have posted private property signs at start of this secondary dirt road to the NW, but it's not true (and you won't pass any homes). Just stay on the road which leads to a trailhead and walk past the gate onto a closed road N.

name: S2 1.7 - JR
landmark: Join closed road (N). There are some signs around here giving the impression the area is private but it's not.

name: S2 3.0 - XR
landmark: Cross NM 126 onto another gated road / trail (N).

name: S2 8.6 - JL
landmark: road jct (N)

name: S2 9.3 - XR
landmark: Cross FR 144 and join trail / closed road (W).

name: S2 11.8 - JL
landmark: Left (N) and then left again (W) between moto trails and on to FR 314.

name: S2 17.6 - W0
landmark: Calaveras Well (SW of road) (dry)

name: S2 21.0 - JR
landmark: jct / FR 103 (N)

name: S2 22.3 - JL
landmark: jct / FR 70 (NW)

name: S2 25.2 - JR
landmark: Right (W) onto lesser track

name: S2 25.9 - RD>XC
landmark: Leave 2-track approx here and head S up to ridge in search of next wpt at terminus of another road.

name: S2 26.0 - XC>RD
landmark: join road (W)

name: S2 27.1 - TH JL
landmark: Impromptu trailhead. Leave 2-track NW onto Penas Negras Tr 32, soon entering San Pedro Parks Wilderness. Trail is little-used and featured semi-frequent blowdowns in 2017 requiring short diversions to avoid. Reportedly has received at least some trail maintenance since then. (CCW join road S.)

name: S2 28.1 - !!
landmark: 2017 this area was really slow going for over a mile due to lots of deadfall and bushwhacking and vague or obliterated trail.

name: S2 30.9 - JL
landmark: Possible obscure junction with Vega Redonda Tr. Remain on Penas Negras Tr.

name: S2 33.5 - JL
landmark: Jct with Highline Tr 385. Remain on Penas Negras Tr (W).

name: S2 43.5 - XR
landmark: CDT / NNML crosses graded FR 103

name: S2 46.8 - XR
landmark: CDT / NNML crosses paved NM Hwy 96. Coyote, 7 mi E, has a post office, church, and maybe has (or maybe used to have) a small c-store.

name: S2 52.3 - TR>RD
landmark: CDT / NNML joins jeep road (NE).

name: S2 52.4 - JL
landmark: Jeep road jct, left (N).

name: S2 54.2 - JL RD>TR

name: S2 60.1 - !!

name: S2 60.9 - JL TH
landmark: Ojitos Trailhead. CDT / NNML leaves FR 151 (N) back onto constructed singletrack. Big climb, big views, much preferred to the sometimes busy (and dusty) dirt road along Rio Chama. Stock up on water first. Possibly nothing else until Ghost Ranch. Re-enter Chama River Canyon Wilderness.

name: S2 64.1 - !!
landmark: CDT reaches mesa rim. Exit Chama River Canyon Wilderness for last time.

name: S2 69.3 - TJ (S2A START)
landmark: Main route of NNML leaves official CDT, joining Rim Vista Tr 15 (S). This is the start of standard CDT hiker's Ghost Ranch resupply route, allowing for a walk-in resupply. NNML hikers not needing Ghost Ranch can opt to remain on official CDT (what we call our CDT Ghost Ranch Bypass). Only recommended if truly needing to bypass G.R., such as if the private retreat happens to be closed to hikers, as it was during the early stages of Covid.

name: S2 71.8 - TH TR>RD
landmark: Trail 15 lower trailhead. Continue SE on TH access road.

name: S2 72.2 - JL JR
landmark: Brief left (NE) on graded FR 151, then right (E) on jeep road.

name: S2 73.3 - JL
landmark: 4-way two track junction, left (E).

name: S2 73.6 - JR
landmark: Right (S) on paved NM 84, passing the now defunct Ghost Ranch Living Museum, a former wildlife exhibit (basically a small zoo) operated by the forest service.

name: S2 73.9 - JL
landmark: The old trail from the museum is blocked off with threatening signs and barbed wire now, but there's a gate around here somewhere that skirts the property and you can then pick up the rest of the trail to Ghost Ranch Conference Center (aka your resupply point) without incident. Remember that Ghost Ranch is private property ahead, so no camping outside of the lodging options offered on campus.

name: S2 75.3 - JR
landmark: E end of connector trail. Join graded road (E) rest of way to Ghost Ranch.

name: S3 0.5 - TH
landmark: Trail / road continues straight from end of Ghost Ranch complex. (Ignore side trail for Kitchen Mesa and continue on closed road west of Arroyo del Yeso drainge.)

name: S3 1.2 - JL
landmark: Ghost Ranch CDT Alt (trail) leaves drainage (N).

name: S3 3.6 - !!
landmark: Trail drops to cross Arroyo del Yeso drainage.

name: S3 4.2 - !!
landmark: Trail / 2-track returns to Arroyo del Yeso drainage and continues along it (NE).

landmark: Leave Arroyo del Yeso impromptu social trail and head x-c (E) up slope.

name: S3 6.4 - XC>RD
landmark: Pick up old 2-track road along mesa top approx here (E).

name: S3 7.0 - JL
landmark: Pick up better road (FR 139 or 138) NW of Yeso Tank.

landmark: CDT leaves FR 724 back onto singletrack (W).

landmark: CDT leaves graded county road onto lesser road (N)

landmark: CDT probably becomes singletrack again to NW (somewhere around here, if it isn't already)

name: S3 28.3 - JR (TR>RD)
landmark: CDT joins graded FR 129 (N). This area has had relo's in place recent years due to aspen die-off from drought and caterpillar infestation along with resulting blowdowns. Keep eyes peeled for any related signs.

name: S3 28.4 - TH (RD>TR)
landmark: Trailhead with vault toilet near Upper Canjilon Lakes campground.

name: S3 31.0 - JR (TR>RD)
landmark: CDT joins 2-track road network (E) (if it hasn't already)

name: S3 31.2 - JR (RD>TR)
landmark: Constructed singletrack eases burden of climb along road.

name: S3 31.7 - JR (TR>RD)
landmark: JR (TR>RD)

name: S3 33.7 - JL (RD>TR)
landmark: CDT singletrack (NE) just after cattleguard.

landmark: JL (TR>RD)

landmark: CDT singletrack crosses FR 274

landmark: CDT crosses US Hwy 64, through gate, onto 2-track (NW)

landmark: CDT (old 2-track) reaches junction of two vehicle roads. Follow better road N.

name: S3 58.2 - JR
landmark: Old road junction. Right (N).

name: S3 59.6 - JR
landmark: 2-track jct (N)

name: S3 62.3 - XR (RD>TR)
landmark: Cross (or leave) 2-track. Singletrack CDT (NE). Waypoint and track just ahead may be approximate.

name: S3 62.9 - JL
landmark: Singletrack CDT passes through gate (N).

name: S3 66.0 - !! (JR)
landmark: Don't stay along fence (CW). In 2017 it was all too easy to keep following use-trail this direction. CDT / NNML turns SE, switchbacks down and along drainage.

name: S3 71.1 - XR (TR>RD)
landmark: CDT crosses graded FR 87, joining FR 87B.

name: S3 72.3 - JL CG (RD>TR)
landmark: CDT leaves Upper Lagunitas CG back to singletrack (NW).

name: S3 74.8 - XR
landmark: CDT singletrack crosses FR 87.

name: S4 4.2 - JR
landmark: Join use-trail (E) near confluence Beaver Creek and Cruces Creek.

name: S4 4.4 - JR
landmark: Leave main use-trail (S) to head up drainage of Diablo Creek. May find remnants of trail although hike is XC in spirit.

name: S4 7.3 - GT
landmark: Gate in fence / xc or faint swath to E

name: S4 8.3 - TH JR
landmark: Road fork by corral / impromptu trailhead for Cruces Basin Wilderness. CW head SE.

name: S4 8.5 - XR (RD>XC)
landmark: Cross graded FR 877 and continue E on faint swath to xc down side drainage / head of Canon Largo.

name: S4 13.6 - GT !!
landmark: Small remote, private inholding here, gated on both ends though not hard to get inside. East end has sign telling public to detour around to the south. Could try walking fenceline or take your chances - get the impression they are trying to prevent vehicle abuses mostly. At last check there was nothing going on inside, and the west side is basically wide open. It DOES keep the upper canyon largely pristine!

name: S4 13.7 - GT !!
landmark: East end of private inholding.

name: S4 15.9 - JL
landmark: Graded road junction at corral. (Corral is private - please don't access for water, etc.) Head E on FR 93. No camping due to several private inholdings / ranches until next waypoint.

name: S4 18.2 - JR !!
landmark: FR 93 at FR 87. Remain straight. Re-enter public lands (E).

name: S4 19.8 - RD>XC
landmark: Leave FR 78 approx here and head xc or via faint swath NE generally up wide drainage meadow. Roads shown on USFS base map this direction don't really exist anymore.

name: S4 20.9 - GT !!
landmark: Should be a gate in fence here. Continue xc SE along edge of trees ahead (for guidance).

name: S4 21.5 - !!
landmark: Scenic xc travel continues along rim of Rio San Antonio, now in an increasingly desert-like volcanic canyon.

name: S4 24.5 - GT XR
landmark: XC or 'cattle drive' route up from Rio San Antonio reaches FR 118 at open gate. Cross this road onto lesser FR 118B (E), ascending gradually at base of San Antonio Mtn.

name: S4 25.2 - JR
landmark: Junction of 2-track roads. CW head S. Nearby Game Depot Tank on map is almost always dry.

name: S4 25.6 - JL
landmark: Another faint 2-track junction. CW head E.

name: S4 25.9 - TH !!
landmark: There's an incredibly well-built trail ascending San Antonio Mtn that begins in this general area. We weren't able to find its precise terminus, though it likely is a natural continuation of the 2-track road here, so head E up the canyon bottom for now. If you can't find it once in the trees, aim for the next waypoint instead, which is where we picked up the trail by complete accident (switchbacking uphill from the north). If you're here and reading this, please mark a waypoint at actual trail start or better yet record a track if you possibly can). It was wonderful to be able to climb the steep slopes of San Antonio Mtn via good switchbacking trail instead of entirely xc as on first attempt.

name: S4 26.1 - !!
landmark: We found the trail here (E). This obviously old but well-built trail has withstood the test of time, yet appears on no base maps that we could find. Perhaps it's a sheepherder's trail, as that has historically been a primary use of the mountain (and remains so to an extent).

name: S4 27.3 - TR>XC
landmark: Trail suddenly ended and we picked up easy-to-follow flagging tape through the forest. Eventually the tape seemed to be heading us in the wrong direction so we left it and made our own way xc up and over forested saddle to San Antonio Mtn Tank.

name: S4 28.6 - JR
landmark: 2-track at graded FR 418 (SE). Road receives mostly 'official use' related to antenna array on summit, but is a public road on public land so hold your ground if receiving any strange looks (see next waypoint for reference).

name: S4 28.9 - GT
landmark: Believe a gate is here along road, possibly with signs stating no public access to east, but this is still USFS land for now. However, hikers should leave the road at next waypoint (E) to avoid private property farther ahead.

name: S4 29.0 - RD>XC
landmark: Leave road (CW) for adventurous xc (SE). Road ahead leaves USFS land for private (part of community of homes on NE slopes of mountain). Road is posted no trespassing beyond public boundary, and is enforceable so please don't do that. Waypoint here is convenient spot to proactively leave road. Our route down mountain - all public - is entirely xc, steep at times but generally open enough to make your own switchbacks.

name: S4 29.5 - !!
landmark: Enter state land (SE). Don't stray NE into private.

name: S4 30.7 - XR
landmark: Cross 2-track, possibly a fence or two or three between here and highway ahead. Enter BLM-administered Rio Grande del Norte National Monument.

name: S4 31.3 - Waypoint S4 31.3
landmark: Unlocked gate in wire fence. (Might still have to negotiate non-gated fences before arriving here, but at least it's something.) Then cross busy 2-lane US Hwy 285 and (slightly N) pass through unlocked metal gate onto unsigned 2-track road (E). On the chance you'd like to try your luck hitching, the tiny crossroads community of Tres Piedras is 13mi S, with PO and a restaurant, plus an RTD bus stop (to Taos).

name: S4 31.9 - JR
landmark: Unsigned 2-track junction (S).

name: S4 34.5 - RD>XC
landmark: Begin extended stretch of wide-open cross-country E / SE and down off low ridge. (CCW aim for ridge and this wpt or next wpt for your direction of travel.)

name: S4 35.2 - !!
landmark: Cross N-S fence without gate. Should be a negotiable spot near waypoint here.

name: S4 36.8 - XR
landmark: Cross N-S 2-track road and continue on your W-E xc trajectory.

name: S4 37.3 - !!
landmark: Another N-S fence without the benefit of a gate.

name: S4 37.7 - JR (XR>RD)
landmark: End of extended cross-country at a lonely 2-track road. Head right / S. (CCW there may be a faint swath heralding the start of what is essentially all xc travel W as you leave the main 2-track here.)

name: S4 39.3 - GT JL
landmark: Pass through gate. 2-track jct by fence corner (E).

name: S4 39.9 - XR
landmark: Cross another 2-track. Don't pass through gate to N. Continue with E-W fenceline.

name: S4 41.3 - JL JR
landmark: At cattleguard, pass through W-E fence and continue on same trajectory. Follow fence swath. (CW you're now on the N side of the fence. CCW you're on the S side.)

name: S4 41.8 - JL XC
landmark: After crossing brushy drainage, leave fence and head N / NE xc on same trajectory as drainage.

name: S4 42.1 - !!
landmark: Cross drainage (CW to N side).

name: S4 42.8 - !!
landmark: Join main drainage E (Arroyo Punche), still essentially xc.

name: S4 43.8 - JL (XC>RD)
landmark: Cross-country definitively ends at junction of well-defined roads. Follow road through cattleguard here (E-SE). (CCW if you need water due to last source being unexpectedly dry, can continue N on graded road toward water waypoint several miles to N (solar well with 2 troughs.)

name: S4 45.1 - JL
landmark: Left / E onto lesser road, then right (SE) toward water waypoint / important stock source in this dry volcanic plateau country.

name: S4 45.6 - JR
landmark: Road junction S, then straight through next junction just ahead.

name: S4 46.9 - JL
landmark: 4-way jct. Pass through cattleguard and head E on lesser (fenceline) track (CW).

name: S4 47.3 - JR
landmark: Leave fenceline track SE onto another 2-track.

name: S4 48.2 - XR
landmark: 4-way jct. Continue straight (SE).

name: S4 49.3 - JR
landmark: 2-track jct. Continue straight (SE).

name: S4 49.7 - !!
landmark: Complex of roads. Track is accurate. At waypoint, head S on this road (not on base map).

name: S4 50.4 - JL
landmark: T-junction with better road (head E).

name: S4 51.2 - JL
landmark: Road junction. Keep straight (SE).

name: S4 51.4 - JR
landmark: Road junction, right fork (E-NE).

name: S4 52.1 - JR
landmark: Road junction. Right fork (SE).

name: S4 52.8 - TH (RD>TR)
landmark: Impromptu trailhead for steep, rough trail into Rio Grande Gorge at Sheep Crossing. Historic trail used by sheepherders, more recently by ranchers to move livestock (still more recently by elk, who appear to be the primary user group other than the very occasional Northern Looper). (Historic footbridge over Rio Grande no longer exists.)

name: S4 53.0 - TH (TR>RD)
landmark: End of trail from Sheep Crossing of Rio Grande. Toilet and table here (no water). (CCW if you find the Rio too dangerous to ford, then you may need to backtrack to Cerro or Questa and take an RTD bus around. Bus stops in village of Arroyo Hondo, then hike or hitch down to familiar John Dunn Bridge across Rio, then head N on Highwater Bypass. Can connect steps, minus river crossing, then continue W on main route. But hopefully - usually - you shouldn't have to do this, esp if packing a raft in times of known high water)

name: S4 53.1 - JL JR
landmark: Left (NE) just briefly onto paved Wild Rivers Backcountry Byway, then right (S) on dirt road. (Or hitch S to main Wild Rivers Rec Area with visitor center and interpretive trails overlooking some of the most dramatic portions of Rio Grande Gorge.)

name: S4 53.6 - JR
landmark: Continue with main road (S).

name: S4 54.3 - JL
landmark: 2-track jct (SE).

name: S4 54.9 - TJ (JL)
landmark: Road meets Las Vistas de Questa Trail at cairn. CW remain on road SE (which is also the trail.) (CCW remain on the road, leaving the marked trail here.) (Special note: CW or CCW hiker could instead follow trail W and S to Wild Rivers Rec Area visitor center and overlook trails, although the main route offers plenty to admire as well in the course of the long journey.)

name: S4 54.9 - RD>TR
landmark: Las Vistas de Questa Tr becomes singletrack (E).

name: S4 57.1 - TJ
landmark: Short side trail E to excellent overlook of Questa and Sangre de Cristo Mountains ahead on our route. Vistas - hey it's the trail's namesake feature!

name: S4 57.4 - TJ
landmark: Another vista point is S here via short side trail.

name: S4 57.5 - JR
landmark: Trail fork (CW head NE). (NW forms a loop trail with our main route, we think.)

name: S4 58.1 - !!
landmark: Boundary Rio Grande del Norte National Monument. Private (and town) lands to E (remain on trail / no camping).

name: S4 58.8 - TH JL
landmark: Las Vistas de Questa trailhead. Leaving parking lot, head S on graded dirt.

name: S4 59.5 - JL
landmark: E on Gonzales Rd

name: S5 0.7 - JR
landmark: Leave ATV swath alongside NM Hwy 522 onto dirt Ed Road (E). Of note - Taos Bakes (formerly Taos Mountain) energy bar company has its main production facility across the highway here. Do they offer walk-in samples or tours?

name: S5 1.1 - JL
landmark: Ed Road at unnamed dirt track to N. Hopefully not signed against public use.

name: S5 1.2 - JR
landmark: Unsigned dirt track at paved Llano Rd.

name: S5 2.5 - JL
landmark: Llano Rd at Cabresto Rd. CW E on Cabresto Rd, which becomes graded dirt and soon enters Carson National Forest (camping privileges return, although a good portion of the road is surrounded with private inholdings). (CCW note that Cabresto Rd continues into Questa, however this route isn't really recommended due to many loose dogs and a general sense of unease approaching town this direction).

name: S5 8.0 - TH (RD>TR)
landmark: Lake Fork Tr 82 trailhead at Cabresto Lake. No specific trail name reference on signs. Modern vault toilet. Picnic table(s).

name: S5 12.8 - JR
landmark: Signed and cairned junction with Trail 88. Remain on Tr 85 (NE). Route ahead is above treeline and fully exposed to the elements until final descent to Heart Lake.

name: S5 13.3 - TJ
landmark: Something of a trail junction here. Remain on Tr 85. (The other trail might get a person down into the forest during a weather emergency.)

name: S5 14.6 - !!
landmark: Route is marked with large cairns especially E of here (tread may be faint to non-existent at times). This is also closest / most practical approach point to Latir Peak (Latir Mesa high point) (to NW) for anyone interested in attaining such things (offers a commanding view north toward 14'er Blanca Peak in Colorado!) Also of possible note: Latir Lakes, attractive series of tarns to NE, are located on private lands (no access without prior arrangements with Rio Costilla Co-op).

name: S5 15.4 - !!
landmark: Visible / constructed trail returns here at edge of Latir Mesa, switchbacking off to the south. (CCW, cairns lead the way ahead NW.)

name: S5 16.7 - JL
landmark: Signed junction of trails 85 / 82 / 81. CW, keep straight onto Midnight Trail 81. Baldy Cabin via side trail ahead. Worth a look, maybe not a night's sleep. (CCW, right fork onto Tr 85. Don't skip Latir Peak Wilderness unless weather forces your hand. And at least visit Heart Lake before bailing out down the alternate.)

name: S5 18.2 - !!
landmark: Signed Wilderness boundary along Tr 81 (exit Latir Peaks Wild. E).

name: S5 20.8 - XT
landmark: Trail/road crosses fenceline swath

name: S5 21.4 - JR
landmark: Tr 81 leaves road back to singletrack (right fork). (CCW trail becomes old road for a bit.)

name: S5 24.1 - JR (RD>XC) (S5A START)
landmark: Lagunita Saddle. FR 134 at FR 54C. Main route leaves road here, heading xc E along north side of Bitter Creek drainage. (CCW join FR 134 at road bend and head W.) This junction is also the start of the all-new partially- unvetted Valle Vidal Alternate (continuing N on FR 134). This adventurous alt (a semi out-and-back solution for the Northern Loop's former / problematic alignment east of here) tours many of the unique highlights of the Valle Vidal Unit of Carson NF, but pack an adventurous attitude, and only attempt during late summer or autumn (when spring / seasonal elk calving public access restrictions are not in place). 28 mi alt (27 mi longer than main route) lacks a set of waypoints at this time but normally has water in major drainages and within Valle Vidal itself. Full track is shown on Avenza and Gaia mapping resources.

name: S5 25.3 - !! (XC>RD) (S5A END)
landmark: Junction by Anchor Mine (ruins). Main route continues down Bitter Creek drainage (S), joining faint decommissioned road here (gate to W normally keeps ATV traffic from Red River at bay). This is also the end of the Valle Vidal Alt, mentioned in a previous waypoint (which uses another decomm'd road from NE).

name: S5 29.4 - GT JR (RD>TR)
landmark: Private property ahead! CW the old decommissioned forest / mining road reaches a gate, likely warning against remaining on the road ahead. For the next mile, this road forms the northern boundary of a private inholding surrounded by Carson NF land. Private lands are owned by a various homeowners (some likely gun-nuttier than others) in a rural neighborhood setting. Thankfully there's a signed 'private property bypass' trail hereabouts, directing users onto singletrack NW of the gate and remaining within public land around the problematic section of road ahead. Track of bypass trail is hand-drawn for now and approximate.

name: S5 29.5 - JL
landmark: Somewhere around here should be a junction of so-called Summit Trail and the main private property bypass trail we've been following. Remain on the bypass trail. Drawn track remains approximate.

name: S5 29.8 - TH (TR>RD)
landmark: Somewhere around here the private property bypass trail ends by a sign, at which point we attempt to rejoin the road within the private neighbhorhood (W). In theory the road is open to the public (again, heading west), or at least some sub-set of the public. Our impression is homeowners discouraging motorized use within the neighborhood, with foot use permitted along the road. If for some reason your presence appears unwelcome, suggestion would be to head back N up the hillside a short ways, squarely within public land there, and follow an xc countour westward. (CCW if you made it here along the neighborhood road, look for signed start of private property bypass trail ahead, as main road becomes definitively off-limits to the public ahead.)

name: S5 30.2 - GT !!
landmark: End of private property at vehicle gate. Gate appears to be variably open or closed, and in recent times at least was signed against motorized public use but not closed to pedestrians. In any case, CW pass through the gate (or drop down from your xc perch, if that's what you opted for instead of the road to this point), safely back to Bitter Creek Rd beyond all gates. So long, ornery second-homesteaders. (CCW A gate here likely tells the story of the vehicle-recreating public's non- admittance / persona-non-grata, etc. See next wpt for you, providing more details. To wit, follow road ahead if you're feeling a good vibe, or head N up the hillside onto public land and xc to next waypoint.)

name: S5 32.4 - !!
landmark: Bitter Creek Rd graded dirt becomes paved (SW).

name: S5 32.6 - JR JL
landmark: Bitter Creek Rd at High St. Welcome to Red River. A quick right on High, then left on street named Gilt Edge Tr will get you to NM 38, the main drag in town.

name: S5 33.0 - PO
landmark: Red River post office

name: S6 0.1 - JR TH
landmark: Trailhead Red River Nature Trail south of NM 38 via Pioneer Trail (road) and River St. Trail begins next to base of ski lift and provides a quiet, forested exit from town (CW).

name: S6 1.8 - JR
landmark: FR 486 at paved NM 578. CW head S.

name: S6 3.5 - !!
landmark: Columbine-Hondo Wilderness boundary

name: S6 5.0 - Large meadow with camping prospects
landmark: Large meadow with camping prospects

name: S6 9.6 - TJ (S6A START) (W0)
landmark: Carsonite post in large cairn at top of switchbacks. End of Goose Creek Tr 65 at nexus of trails. Somewhat confusing on map, and only the main route ahead (W) is really visible on the ground here above treeline. This cairn also represents start of highly recommended Wheeler Peak Loop Alternate. Alt adds 18 mi to distance between Red River and San Cristobal resupply points. (Begin alt by heading S, effectively xc at first, then on visible singletrack tread heading into forest - Gold Creek Tr 64.)

name: S6 9.8 - TJ (S6A END) (W0)
landmark: Signed trail junction. End of 18-mi Wheeler Peak Loop Alt. CW head N on Gold Hill Tr 64.

name: S6 9.9 - JL
landmark: Cairn here at junction Gold Hill Tr 64 and (faint) Lobo Peak Tr 57 (NW) which we join (CW). Tr 57 generally follows crest of broad ridge above cirque of Long Canyon.

name: S6 11.3 - !!
landmark: Unnamed Peak 12217 on Lobo Peak Tr.

name: S6 14.2 - !!
landmark: Trail 57 atop unnamed peak 11849 following sharp ascent (this portion of trail along ridge seems like quite an engineering feat to have constructed, and perhaps explains the need to go right over the top here).

name: S6 15.9 - JR
landmark: TJ / Lobo Peak Trl 57 and Italianos Cyn Tr 59. Keep straight (S) on Tr 57

name: S6 17.4 - JR
landmark: Signed jct Lobo Peak Tr 57 and Yerba Canyon Tr 61. Remain on Tr 57.

name: S6 17.5 - !! (TR>XC)
landmark: Lobo Peak summit. Trail 57 continues obscurely N from here. Essentially xc here to next waypoint (CW), dropping and then contouring just W of main ridge. (CCW pick up trail at or below summit of Lobo Peak.)

name: S6 17.8 - !!
landmark: CW head up linear meadow here (N). Trail tread should resume by next waypoint.

name: S6 17.8 - !! (XC>TR)
landmark: Lobo Peak Tr visible tread resumes to N, heading into forest. Trail remains little-used and occasionally obscure but generally follows (or quickly returns to) crest of forested ridge. Recorded track is not 100% accurate due to our losing the trail sporadically.

name: S6 19.1 - JL C
landmark: Signed jct Lobo Peak Tr 57 and San Cristobal Canyon Tr 78. CW leave ridge crest here onto Tr 78, with visible tread. Potential campsite at junction. (CCW head S onto Tr 57, with occasionally obscure tread especially south of next saddle.)

name: S6 21.1 - C W3
landmark: Potential campsite opposite meadow. (Frequent creek crossings in narrow canyon below here along trail, minimizing good camping potential.)

name: S6 24.4 - TH (TR>RD)
landmark: Trailhead for San Cristobal Canyon Tr 78 just beyond Columbine-Hondo Wilderness boundary. Join rough dirt FR 439C (SW).

name: S6 24.8 - !!
landmark: FR 439C leaves Carson NF and public land (SW), becomes Camino del Medio. No camping here to San Cristobal village.

name: S6 25.5 - !!
landmark: Camino del Medio becomes graded dirt (SW).

name: S7 0.4 - JL
landmark: Graded dirt road junction, Camino del Medio at Old Hwy 3. NNML heads S (or to take a scheduled RTD blue bus to Questa or Taos, head N then W [via FR 493] out to NM Hwy 522 for the roadside bus stop, 0.8 mi total off-route).

name: S7 0.6 - They have a guest laundry on site, but no food (other than eggs and goji
landmark: Taos Goji Eco Lodge is on the right. May have cabins on short notice. They have a guest laundry on site, but no food (other than eggs and goji berries from the farm seasonally). www.taosgoji.com

name: S7 1.2 - JR
landmark: Old Hwy 3 ends at junction with wide graded Lawrence Ranch Rd (aka San Cristobal Rd aka CR B009). Continue W.

name: S7 2.1 - XR !!
landmark: Signed County Road B009 ends at NM Hwy 522. Cross highway to roadside pull-out and find de facto (moto) trail heading up bermed embankment here (NW). Then join the main 2-track road above the berm (turns SW, as shown on USFS base map). Solidly within USFS lands again here, no camping restrictions (CCW don't camp heading E to San Cristobal).

name: S7 2.2 - JR
landmark: CW take the right-most road option here at unsigned jct, heading W.

name: S7 2.7 - XR !!
landmark: 4 way 2-track junction. Head W and soon cross from USFS into BLM Rio Grande del Norte National Monument without fanfare.

name: S7 3.5 - JL
landmark: 2-track junction. Keep left (SW).

name: S7 3.6 - JR
landmark: Singletrack Horsethief Mesa Trail crosses road but don't join it here (we'll join it ahead at another spot on its loop journey). Continue short distance S to waypoint, 2-track junction, and take the right fork (SW).

name: S7 3.7 - JL
landmark: Another 2-track junction (SW).

name: S7 3.9 - JR
landmark: 2-track jct (WNW). (Ignore another jct just before here with fainter road that heads NNW).

name: S7 4.1 - JL (RD>TR)
landmark: Join Horsethief Mesa Loop Tr here (S). Purpose-built (mtb-oriented) loop trail, with our half (the western half) arcing sinuously near rim of Rio Grande Gorge with its familiar, superlative scenery.

name: S7 7.2 - JR (TR>RD)
landmark: Horsethief Mesa Tr joins 2-track (S). (Side note: although it's possible to take a shorter route across this terrain via combinations of old road, the marked trail is more rewarding and less navigationally involving.)

name: S7 7.5 - JR (RD>TR)
landmark: Horsethief Mesa Tr continues S from road bend, back to singletrack.

name: S7 8.1 - JR
landmark: Cairned trail junction. CW right fork (E). (CCW may be tempting to head NE here, seemingly more efficient, but trail becomes confused by road network and getting back on track is actually less efficient.)

name: S7 9.1 - TR>XC
landmark: Leave Horsethief Mesa Loop Trail (SE) here where it reaches road at bend and proceed xc downhill through juniper forest. Our goal is to connect the trail with bridged crossing of Rio Gande via CR B 007 down below rim of mesa. Proceed to next wpt for a reasonable way off the volcanic rim.

name: S7 9.2 - XC !!
landmark: Start of steep rocky descent off rim of Horsethief Mesa. Track is route we took, which was reasonable if somewhat slow-going. Non-technical.

name: S7 9.3 - JR (XC>RD)
landmark: At the base of your adventurous descent, tie a bow on the rugged experience by crossing the flowing drainage of Arroyo Hondo. Then climb up to graded road (CR B-007) and head west. (CCW leave road here at waypoint, cross the creek, then find a line up to the rim. This is the first reasonable point to make such an ascent for you, coming from the Rio.)

name: S7 9.8 - Dunn Bridge W3
landmark: John Dunn Bridge over Rio Grande

sheetComments.author: Mike Unger
sheetComments.text: Bridge is closed until mid Nov, 22. It is probably impassable if construction crews are present. Road on either side is also closed with a new barbed wire fence and “no trespassing “ sign. Hikers should consider the rio grande high water route during closure.

sheetComments.author: Brett T
sheetComments.text: The high water route is intended for clockwise hikers unable to ford the Rio west of Questa, relying on this bridged crossing in an emergency. So the high water route can’t serve its intended purpose right now with the bridged closure. Thinking a hiker reaching the bridge would need to attempt the ford here instead or backtrack if dangerous. For a clockwise hiker this might mean finding a ride to Taos Gorge bridge and resuming the route there.

sheetComments.author: Kyle Morris
sheetComments.text: Thanks Brett. Looks like I'll be fording or swimming at the original fording location.

sheetComments.author: Brett T
sheetComments.text: Kyle I think what it means is basically an extra required ford, first the usual one west of Questa, and then fording at Dunn Bridge after you exit the Columbine-Hondo and are on your way to Ranchos de Taos. However, what about crossing the bridge early AM or after 6 PM or whatever to avoid the construction crew.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Oct 2023. Bridge is open and there are vault toilets. Road up the west side of the canyon is accessible to foot traffic, but blocked to vehicles. I didn't check out the Black Rock Hot Springs just south on the east side.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Toilets, trash, shade. What else could you want?

name: S7 10.9 - JL (RD>XC) (S4B END)
landmark: Leave graded CR B-007 at small side drainage and head xc up the rocky drainage itself or adjacent. This is also the (S) end of the Rio Grande Highwater Bypass, affording a bridged crossing of the river.

name: S7 11.1 - JL (XC>RD)
landmark: Leave drainage and head S on 2-track road. (CCW join rocky drainage here in order to make connection down toward graded road below to E.)

name: S7 11.6 - JL
landmark: Jeep road junction (S).

name: S7 12.2 - JR
landmark: Jeep road junction, right fork (SW).

name: S7 12.6 - !!
landmark: Old fence across the road here at bend. We were able to peel back a wire or two and proceed (no warning signs, all BLM).

name: S7 12.8 - JR
landmark: Road junction right (W).

name: S7 13.6 - RD>XC
landmark: Leave the 2-track road around here and head SE to avoid private lands ahead, (old quarry and such) which we'll walk around on a boundary line. Our route remains within Rio Grande del Norte N.M.

name: S7 13.8 - !!
landmark: Wide gap in N-S fenceline here. Our xc route then proceeds S, in the spirit of following this fenceline but at whatever distance away from it as dictated by terrain, wash crossings, etc.

name: S7 15.1 - !!
landmark: Fence corner. Use as a guidepost of sorts as xc route turns toward SW here, now leaving fenceline corridor and negotiating some occasionally rather dense sagebrush in pursuit of next objective, a feasible way across side canyon of the Rio ahead.

name: S7 15.9 - !!
landmark: We headed down off the rim into the Rio side canyon here. Nothing technical about the drop into this dry rocky drainage, at least not here. This is just outside of the national monument boundary, a very minor indiscretion it seemed for a feasible way across. Once in drainage, we headed down it a little ways and then picked our way back up and out. General rule: the canyon becomes deeper, steeper, and more difficult to negotiate the closer to the main gorge, so don't keep heading down the drainage too long.

name: S7 16.0 - !!
landmark: Back atop the side canyon, back within the monument, heading S on a continued xc course. (CCW you'll probably want to tackle this canyon by first heading W along rim for a bit as the way down in becomes more reasonable that direction.)

name: S7 16.8 - !! XR (END XC)
landmark: We found portion of E-W fence that was down here (just E of N-S fence and private boundary). Head on through then carefully cross busy Hwy 64 into Rio Grande Gorge Bridge rest area. Your extended stint of xc travel ends here.

name: S7 17.5 - JL
landmark: Carsonite posted trail junction directs you S, but go left (SE) for best views of gorge bridge, even a nifty bench at the edge of the abyss. Route meets back up again.

name: S7 18.1 - JL
landmark: Unsigned jct along West Rim Tr. Take the singletrack option (S).

name: S7 18.8 - JL
landmark: Cairned junction. Trail heads left (S) onto double-track.

name: S7 23.1 - JL
landmark: West Rim Trail double-track junction. Left fork (SE).

name: S7 24.1 - JL
landmark: West Rim Tr resumes its south-trending trajectory here after a brief westerly jaunt with powerlines.

name: S7 26.0 - !!
landmark: Signed side trail to Utopian Vista. CW you've seen a lot of the Rio Grande Gorge to date but you know you have to check it out.

name: S7 26.5 - TH JR
landmark: West Rim Tr passes adjacent to trailhead (remains singletrack, turns W here, doesn't enter parking lot).

name: S7 26.6 - XR
landmark: Carsonite singletrack crossing of W Rim TH entrance road.

name: S7 26.9 - JL (TR>RD)
landmark: Signed terminus of West Rim Trail at NM 567. Head left (SE) on this graded dirt road down toward another bridged crossing of the Rio Grande. (Signed Petaca Point Tr is across road here but doesn't serve our purposes.)

name: S7 28.1 - !!
landmark: Signed La Vista Verde TH to N, but our route remains on NM 567.

name: S7 28.9 - JL
landmark: Picuris Trail splits here. CW take left fork (E). We'll climb out of the gorge via another trail ahead.

name: S7 29.3 - JR TH
landmark: Signed end of Picuris Trail. Head E briefly on road to lower trailhead for Slide Trail. This is our route back up to the rim, an old road-turned-trail ascending gradually via side canyon of Rio Grande (which contains the scenic if somewhat hard to access Rio Pueblo, a creek we'll approach again, more leisurely, in the next section of NNML).

name: S7 30.7 - TH JL (TR>RD)
landmark: Upper trailhead for Slide Trail. Popular spot among mtb'ers using new trail network in area (not the Slide Trail so much). Vault toilet here. CW head E on the paved county road (110). You're finally out of the vast Rio Grande del Norte National Monument for good, along with public lands between here and your upcoming town stop at Ranchos de Taos, so no camping allowed ahead. (CCW note that you want to join the Slide Trail here from the TH, which heads N just W of the toilet.)

name: S7 32.9 - JL !!
landmark: You don't want the road heading due N here, but rather the lesser utility road heading NE. Leave CR 110 onto this efficient, if undulating, shortcut of a swath.

name: S7 33.9 - JL
landmark: Utility swath ends at graded dirt Los Cordovas Rd. Join it N. You're now in outskirts-ville of Taos, with uber ranchettes and a golf course dotting the mystical sagebrush plain.

name: S7 35.0 - !!
landmark: Los Cordovas Rd graded dirt becomes pavement to N, adjacent rural fire department (water?)

name: S7 35.2 - JR
landmark: Right (SE) off Los Cordovas Rd onto Cuchilla Rd.

name: S7 37.4 - JL
landmark: Left (SE) off Cuchilla Rd onto Tom Holder Rd. Enter acequia (irrigation district) lands here along with a notable change in scenery from open desert to historic farm and ranch land, treed yards, and the like.

name: S7 37.8 - JR
landmark: Right (SE) onto Los Cordovas Rd (SR 240) again.

name: S8 0.2 - JL JR
landmark: Left (E) from paved San Francisco Rd onto Espinoza Rd, then right (S) on Camino Abajo de la Loma

name: S8 1.6 - JR
landmark: South at end of Camino Abajo onto NM Hwy 518 (sidewalk available).

name: S8 2.2 - JL JR
landmark: Left (E) from Hwy 518 onto FR 437, then in 100 yards right (S) on Monte Lindo Rd.

name: S8 2.3 - JR GT
landmark: Right fork from Monte Lindo leads to solar-powered vehicle gate / private drive with key code. A pedestrian pass-through allows unfettered access, and until we hear otherwise provides a proper connection to neighborhood portal onto public trail network ahead.

name: S8 2.6 - TH JL (RD>TR)
landmark: Left (SE) off neighborhood road onto Rio Grande del Ranchos Trail #18. Trail enters public (USFS) land within 0.1 mi ahead (CCW no camping here to town). Trail features occasional 'Tr 8' markers and wooden signs throughout, and is for the most part an old road converted to pseudo- singletrack.

name: S8 2.9 - JL
landmark: Small cairn at unsigned junction with spur trail from highway below. Stay left (SE) on main Trail 18.

name: S8 8.5 - JR
landmark: Keep south on Trail 18 as another road joins from left. (CCW take left fork to remain on Tr 18.)

name: S8 8.6 - TH (TR>RD)
landmark: Named Trail 18 ends here (S) with carsonite post and 'no vehicles' signs. Remain on the now-driven dirt road S.

name: S8 9.1 - JL
landmark: Leave signed graded dirt FR 438, left (S) on signed FR 440.

name: S8 9.6 - JR
landmark: Right off FR 440 onto unsigned lesser road.

name: S8 10.4 - GT
landmark: Walk around old metal gate. Decommissioned road beyond, essentially a grassy swath.

name: S8 10.5 - JR (RD>TR)
landmark: Cross drainage and return (W) (don't continue E/S). Overgrown road becomes ever more vague approaching creek ahead, eventually use- trails through willow thicket to crossing of Rio Grande del Rancho (creek) at next wpt.

name: S8 10.8 - W3
landmark: First crossing CW of Rio Grande del Rancho (creek in meadow).

name: S8 10.9 - TR>RD
landmark: Use-trails end at guardrail and parking / camping. Join dirt road network S. Large dispersed vehicle-oriented camping area here and beyond.

name: S8 11.1 - JL
landmark: Keep S through unsigned jct as another dirt road joins from right. You're now on FR 439. (CCW take the right fork off FR 439.)

name: S8 13.1 - W3 JR
landmark: CW last access to Rio Grande del Rancho. 2 track jct. Turn right uphill on vague secondary utility line 2track. Utility line gradual at first then very steep.

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Oct 2023. Flowing with several beaver ponds through here

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text: Flowing. Nice big pine nearby to take a shaded rest under. And dry out from walking in and across the river/bog

sheetComments.author: Molly Katzman
sheetComments.text: Beautiful, clear pooled spring flowing from the east side of FR 439, just after joining the road CCW. Better option than the river, what with the abundance of beaver dams 'round here.

sheetComments.author: Aspen Adams
sheetComments.text: Flowing here and at the other crossings. Pay attention because the junction is easy to miss (CW) and it's not a waypoint on the gaia / gpx. The road is subtle and very overgrown. Had to wade through the beaver pond but it was only knee high. I recommend getting water a little bit downstream where it's actually moving though

name: S8 14.2 - JL

name: S8 15.1 - JR JL TH (RD>TR)

name: S8 15.4 - JL
landmark: Left (S) at vague but signed trail jct across meadow. Trail (old logging swath) becomes better defined once it re-enters forest.

name: S8 16.7 - JR
landmark: Trail (old logging road) junction. Sign here refers to 'snowshoe loops.' CW take sharp right (W). (CCW sharp left here, don't take the more northerly tack by mistake.)

name: S8 17.2 - JL
landmark: Signed t-jct, keep left (E) now on Tr 182. Rough and occasionally moto-y. (CCW right fork onto Tr 10.)

name: S8 17.5 - JR
landmark: Unsigned road junction, take the right fork (E).

name: S8 17.8 - JL
landmark: 4 way trail jct. Sorta signed. CW turn left from Trail (logging road) 182 onto Tio Maes Trail 5. Sucky moto singletrack ahead. (Or go right down moto trail in search of spring named Ojito Maes (could not find water 6- 20). (CCW turn right onto Tr 182 / old logging road).

name: S8 18.3 - JL (TR>RD)
landmark: Signed jct end of Tio Maes Tr 5. Turn left (E) on FR 182. (CCW head right onto Tr 5 moto singletrack.)

name: S8 18.6 - JR
landmark: Multi-way road junction. CW turn right (S) at waypoint (not onto road just before). Road ahead (Tr 182) is pleasant mtb-oriented affair, though actually open to all vehicles. (CCW turn left / SW at wpt then keep right.)

name: S8 20.6 - JR (RD>TR)
landmark: Ignore sign for Trail 4 on right and then take the next right off Trail / Road 182 onto signed Flechado Tr 7 singletrack. Occasional nuisance blowdowns ahead 6-20. (CCW Trail 7 ends here; follow road left / N, which is called Trail 182. Ignore signs for Trail 4.)

name: S8 23.3 - Waypoint S8 23.3
landmark: Trailhead for Flechado Trail 7 at NM Hwy 518. Half mile NW to Sipapu Ski and Summer Resort (M L maildrop option). Main route turns left (SE) on highway (parking and vault toilet across the road). Be careful with traffic on this narrow winding 2-lane road.

sheetComments.author: Molly Katzman

name: S8 31.1 - JL (S8A START)
landmark: Signed jct Comales Tr 22 and Divide Tr 36. CW main route heads S on Tr 36, or continue on Tr 22 via Santa Barbara Divide Bypass Route in order to avoid continuous 12 mi ridge above treeline along main route just ahead. The alternate and portion of the main route it replaces are almost exactly the same distance (although alternate loses and then regains several thousand feet of elevation).

sheetComments.author: Will Emmer
sheetComments.text: Oct 2023. At ~0.5 miles there is an abandoned section of forest road that has a spring and some flat ground for a campsite to the west. 36.1N, 105.575W. The new forest road bypasses to the south.

name: S8 32.0 - JL

name: S8 32.8 - !!
landmark: Route above treeline for 12 mi (S). Bail-outs via trail (or xc to treeline) are possible but not always convenient or immediate. Plan for the weather and use Divide Bypass route as needed.

name: S8 33.4 - W2
landmark: Unsigned Jicarita Spring 200 ft W of and below trail from waypoint.

sheetComments.author: Andreas
sheetComments.text:
Found where it looks like a spring should be but no sight or sound of water, despite heavy rain the night before. I did find some run off up hill from the trail 0.1 to the south that I was able to collect from. 
Note that this is labeled as 33.4 in Gaia and 33.2 here.

sheetComments.author: Chris Hoffer

name: S8 70.1 - XT (S8C START)
landmark: Signed jct Skyline / Winsor (again, thanks to convoluted nature of these two trails). Main route remains on Winsor (straight), while Skyline is W and S. (CW, to try out the 5-mile Lake Peak Alternate, offering a scenic conclusion to your time in Pecos Wilderness without much crowd pressure, head S on Skyline here. This high route, however, can hold onto snow quite late into spring or early summer and is somewhat exposed, possibly not the best choice for the less snow-savvy hiker.)

name: S1/8 92.0 - SANTA FE PLAZA
landmark: The Northern New Mexico Loop begins and ends in the center of Santa Fe Plaza. Fine arts, native wares, tchotchkes, and plenty of classy restaurants in the vicinity. The famed Georgia O'Keefe Museum is also nearby. Buena suerte! Good luck on your journey! (And for those having just completed the Loop, congratulations!) To begin clockwise along the Loop, from the plaza head west on San Francisco Street.
